title: The Rota That Works for Everyone
subtitle: A good rota balances business need with human need. Both matter equally.

toc.label: Why the Rota Is a Retention Tool

sections.heading: Why the Rota Is a Retention Tool
sections.body:
The rota is the most personal expression of how you value your team. It determines when they work, when they rest, whether they can attend their child's school play, and whether they know their schedule in time to plan their life.

A rota published on Friday for shifts starting Monday tells the team: your time does not matter to me. A rota published two weeks ahead tells them: I respect that you have a life outside this cafe.

Studies in hospitality consistently show that schedule predictability is one of the top three retention factors — ahead of pay in many cases. People will accept lower wages at a cafe that respects their time over higher wages at one that treats scheduling as an afterthought.

The rota is also a business tool. It determines your labour cost, your peak coverage, and your ability to deliver consistent service. Getting it right is not administrative work — it is strategic work.

sections.heading: Publishing Two Weeks Ahead
sections.body:
**The commitment:** The rota for any given week is published at least 14 days before the start of that week. No exceptions.

**How to make it possible:**

1. **Template-based scheduling.** Most weeks follow a pattern. Create a template rota that covers your standard staffing needs by day and shift. Each week, you are adjusting the template, not building from scratch.

2. **Availability windows.** Each team member provides their standing availability (days/times they can and cannot work). This goes on file and forms the base of every rota.

3. **Holiday and absence requests.** Submitted at least 2 weeks before the rota period. First come, first served. Maximum 1 person on holiday at a time (adjust for team size).

4. **Swap policy.** Team members can swap shifts with each other, provided:
 - Both people agree
 - The manager is notified
 - Both are qualified for the swapped shift
 - It happens before the shift, not during

5. **Rota meeting.** Spend 30 minutes each week building the next rota. Check availability, apply the template, adjust for holidays and events, and publish.

**Communication:** Publish via a shared calendar, scheduling app (Planday, Deputy, When I Work), or a WhatsApp group with a photo of the rota. The method matters less than the consistency.

sections.heading: Exit Interviews and Turnover Analysis
sections.body:
Use T063-01 (Exit Interview Template):

Every person who leaves should have an exit interview. Not a lecture — a conversation. Not on their last day — in their last week, with time to reflect.

**Questions that reveal truth:**

- "What prompted you to start looking for another job?" (The trigger)
- "What did you enjoy most about working here?" (What to protect)
- "What frustrated you most?" (What to fix)
- "If you could change one thing about this cafe, what would it be?" (The priority)
- "Would you recommend this cafe as a place to work?" (The truth test)
- "Is there anything that could have made you stay?" (The missed opportunity)

Record the answers. Review them alongside every other exit interview.

**Turnover analysis:**

Track annually:
- Total leavers ÷ average headcount × 100 = turnover rate
- Voluntary vs involuntary leavers
- Tenure at departure (how long did they stay?)
- Reason for leaving (categorise: pay, hours, management, personal, career, other)

**Industry benchmark:** 40–60% is common in hospitality. Below 30% is excellent. Above 60% indicates a systemic problem.

If most leavers cite the same reason — poor scheduling, lack of development, management style — you have a clear area to address. If reasons vary, the problem may be hiring (bringing in the wrong people) rather than retention.

sections.heading: Compensation and Career Pathways
sections.body:
Use T065-01 (Compensation Benchmark Sheet):

Pay is not the only reason people stay, but it is the first reason they leave. If your wages are below market, no amount of culture will compensate.

**Benchmarking:**

| Role | National Minimum Wage | Local Market Rate | Your Rate | Competitive? |
|------|----------------------|------------------|-----------|-------------|
| Barista | current minimum wage rate | check local rates | | |
| Senior Barista | — | check local rates | | |
| Shift Lead | — | check local rates | | |
| Manager | — | check local rates | | |

Check local job ads quarterly. If your rate is below market, you are losing candidates before you even meet them.

**Beyond hourly rate:**
- Tips and tronc (how are they distributed? Is the policy clear and fair?)
- Staff meals and drinks (a real benefit — budget for it properly)
- Training investment (paid training time, courses, certifications)
- Flexible scheduling (worth more than money to many people)
- Career progression (the prospect of growth is a powerful retention tool)

**Career pathways:**
Use T064-01 to define progression:

Barista → Senior Barista → Shift Lead → Assistant Manager → Manager

Each level should have:
- Clear criteria for promotion (skills, behaviours, tenure)
- A pay increment
- Additional responsibilities and authority
- A title that means something

A barista who can see a path from entry-level pay to shift lead within 18 months has a reason to stay. A barista with no visible path has every reason to look elsewhere.

sections.heading: Protecting Breaks and Preventing Burnout
sections.body:
In a busy cafe, breaks are the first thing to be sacrificed. The rush runs long, someone is late, and suddenly the 11am break becomes the never break.

This is not sustainable. A barista who works 8 hours without a proper break makes mistakes in hour 7 that cost more than 20 minutes of cover. Burns from inattention. Wrong orders from fatigue. Rude interactions from exhaustion.

**Legal minimum:** Workers are entitled to breaks as defined by the national labour code. Check your country's specific requirements for adult and minor employees. These are legal minimums, not targets.

**Best practice:**
- 30-minute paid break for any shift over 5 hours
- Breaks scheduled into the rota, not left to chance
- Break times visible on the shift plan
- Never skip breaks during peak — schedule them before or after the rush

**Burnout signals:**
- Increased sick days
- Declining quality of work
- Withdrawal from team interactions
- Visible fatigue, irritability
- Asking for fewer hours or a change of shift pattern

**Prevention:**
- Limit consecutive closing-then-opening shifts ("clopening") — minimum 11 hours between shifts
- Ensure at least one full day off per week (two is better)
- Monitor weekly hours — consistently exceeding contracted hours is a warning sign
- Check in regularly: "How are you managing? Is the workload okay?"
- Model healthy boundaries as the owner — if you work every day, the team feels guilty about rest

sections.heading: Building a Retention Strategy
sections.body:
Use T064-01 (Retention Action Plan) to build a deliberate strategy:

Retention is not one thing. It is a system of things that together make people want to stay.

**The retention pyramid:**

**Base: Hygiene factors (must have)**
- Fair pay (at or above market)
- Predictable schedule (2 weeks ahead)
- Safe working environment
- Respectful management
- Legal compliance (contracts, breaks, holiday)

If any of these are missing, no amount of culture will compensate. Fix these first.

**Middle: Engagement factors (should have)**
- Regular feedback and one-to-ones
- Training and development opportunities
- Cross-training and variety
- Team meetings and communication
- Recognition and celebration

These are what make the difference between a job and a good job. They cost time, not money.

**Top: Purpose factors (aspire to)**
- Clear career pathway
- Connection to the cafe's mission and values
- Autonomy and decision-making authority
- Community and belonging
- Pride in the product and the workplace

These are what make people say "I love working here" and stay for years.

**Measure retention:**
- Track turnover rate quarterly
- Track average tenure
- Track reason for leaving
- Survey team satisfaction annually (or use pulse checks quarterly)

Set a target: if turnover is 60%, aim for 45% next year. If it is 45%, aim for 30%. Every 10% reduction saves you thousands in recruitment and training costs — and gives your customers the consistency they value.
